special.name: A-Umlaut
special.desc: Like the 'e' in 'bed'. Lips slightly rounded.

special.name: O-Umlaut
special.desc: Say 'eh' with rounded lips. No English equivalent.

special.name: U-Umlaut
special.desc: Say 'ee' with rounded lips. Like French 'u'.

special.name: Eszett / scharfes S
special.desc: Always an 's' sound (never 'z'). Appears after long vowels and diphthongs.

combos.desc: After e/i: soft 'sh'-like hiss (ich). After a/o/u: throaty 'kh' (Bach).
Like English 'sh' in 'ship'. Always this sound.
Sounds like English 'ee' in 'see'. Long vowel.
Same sound as 'eu' — 'oy' in 'boy'. Often a plural marker.
At the start of a word, pronounced 'shp' (not 'sp').
At the start of a word, pronounced 'sht' (not 'st').
Like English 'ck'. Signals the vowel before it is short.
Both letters are pronounced — a quick 'p' then 'f'.
Always 'ts' — like the end of 'cats'. Never a buzzy 'z'.

tips: German W is pronounced like English V: Wasser = 'Vasser'.
German V is usually pronounced like F: Vater = 'Fahter'.
German J sounds like English Y: ja = 'yah'.
Every letter is pronounced — no silent letters (unlike English).
The stress usually falls on the first syllable.
